data.title: Netflix: What ratings and earnings surprise history say ahead of Q4 results
data.description:
As Netflix prepares to report its fourth quarter results, we look at what the company's ratings and earnings surprise history say.

Wall Street analysts are demonstrating strong confidence in the streaming giant. The stock currently carries an average analyst rating of 4.08, translating to a “Buy” recommendation.

Of the 45 analysts who have weighed in over the last 90 days, the sentiment skews decidedly bullish: 22 analysts rate the stock a Strong Buy, while another 8 have assigned a Buy rating. Meanwhile, 13 analysts maintain a Hold position, with just 1 Sell and 1 Strong Sell rating among the group.

Beyond traditional Wall Street coverage, Netflix also receives evaluation through Seeking Alpha’s proprietary rating systems. The platform offers a Quant Rating that employs factor-based analysis, weighting metrics according to their predictive value rather than simply averaging factor grades. The company has a "Hold" recommendation according to SA Quant Rating.

Additionally, Seeking Alpha’s own analysts provide independent ratings on the stock, who also recommend the stock as a "Hold" with a score of 3.39.

Earnings reports added volatility to the tale, with Q3'25 delivering a -15.66% EPS miss against expectations, contrasting earlier beats like Q1'25's 16.58% surprise. YoY growth flickered positively at 8.88% that quarter, but wild swings from -73% lows to 1,708% spikes revealed Netflix's unpredictable script.

data.title: Peoples Bancorp anticipates 3–5% loan growth and NIM of 4–4.2% for 2026 while managing asset threshold
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Peoples Bancorp Inc. (PEBO) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Tyler Wilcox, President and CEO, reported, “Earlier this morning, we reported diluted earnings per share of $0.89 for the fourth quarter which is a 7% increase compared to the linked quarter.” He noted EPS was negatively impacted by $0.02 from the sale of a real estate property and $0.02 from subordinated debt redemption, but emphasized, “For the full year of 2025, we achieved our expected results. We generated positive operating leverage compared to the prior year, excluding the impact of accretion income. We had loan growth of 6% compared to 2024, and our fee-based income improved 6% over the prior year.”
  • Wilcox highlighted the sale of a major OREO property, significantly reducing nonperforming assets, and the redemption of subordinated debt, projecting future funding cost savings. He also stated, “Our book value per share grew 2%, while our tangible book value per share improved by 3% and our diluted earnings per share exceeded consensus analyst estimates for the quarter, which were $0.88.”
  • Major management change was announced: “Earlier this morning, we announced the planned retirement of Doug Wyatt, our Chief Commercial Banking Officer... Stepping into Doug's role will be Ron Majka, who joined us in September and has over 30 years of experience serving middle market companies.”
  • Wilcox addressed strategic focus on the small ticket leasing business, “We are no longer originating these types of leases in our small ticket business. Excluding the lease net charge-offs, the remainder of our loan portfolio had net charge-offs of $2.1 million.”
  • Kathryn Bailey, Executive VP, CFO, stated, “For the fourth quarter, our net interest income was relatively flat when compared to the linked quarter, while our net interest margin declined 4 basis points.” She attributed margin pressure to lower loan yields and described offsetting actions, “We were able to mostly offset declines in our investment and loan income by closely managing our funding costs.”

Outlook

  • Wilcox projected, “We expect to achieve positive operating leverage for 2026 compared to 2025. We anticipate our net interest margin will be between 4% and 4.2% for the full year of 2026, which includes 125 basis point rate cut. Each 25 basis point reduction in rates from the Federal Reserve is expected to result in a 3 to 4 basis point decline in our net interest margin for the full year.”
  • He added, “We believe our quarterly fee-based income will range between $28 million and $30 million. Our first quarter fee-based income is typically elevated as it includes annual performance-based insurance commissions.”
  • Wilcox guided for “quarterly total noninterest expense to be between $72 million and $74 million for the second, third and fourth quarters of 2026, with the first quarter of 2026 being higher due to the annual expenses.”
  • Loan growth is expected at “between 3% and 5% compared to 2025,” influenced by paydown timing and rate environment, with a slight reduction in net charge-offs anticipated for 2026.
  • Management clarified “no plans to go over” the $10 billion asset threshold organically, outlining levers to manage balance sheet size.

Financial Results

  • Wilcox reported, “For the fourth quarter of 2025, our provision for credit losses totaled $8.1 million and was largely driven by net charge-offs.” The allowance for credit losses increased to 1.12% of total loans, with an annualized net charge-off rate of 44 basis points, up from 41 basis points in the linked quarter.
  • Bailey stated, “Our net interest margin was negatively impacted by lower loan yields, which declined 17 basis points, while our overall funding costs declined 10 basis points.” She elaborated on the impact of accretion income and measures to offset margin pressure, including debt redemption.
  • Fee-based income improved 5% quarter-over-quarter, driven by higher lease, deposit account service charges, mortgage banking, and trust and investment income. Noninterest expenses rose 2% due to higher operating lease expense and sales-based compensation, but net interest income for the full year improved 2% over 2024.
  • Tangible equity to tangible assets rose to 8.8%, with most regulatory capital ratios improving. Book value per share at year-end reached $33.78 and tangible book value per share $22.77.

Q&A

  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson: Asked about accretion benefit within margin guide. Bailey replied, “I think 8 is the highest it would be... I would say closer to 5 -- around 5 for the full year.”
  • Rulis asked about tax rate expectations. Bailey responded, “That would get us up to 21.5% on a kind of run rate perspective for '25. And I think you can expect that in -- probably in the 22% range for '26.”
  • Rulis inquired about loan production outlook. Wilcox highlighted, “We have seen incredible demand and incredible execution by our team, particularly led by the commercial team and on the C&I side. And we expect that to continue into this year.”
  • Brendan Nosal, Hovde Group: Questioned $10 billion threshold and M&A criteria. Wilcox reiterated strategic patience, focus on existing/adjacent markets, and a preference for larger deals but openness to smaller acquisitions.
  • Nosal asked about North Star Leasing loss plateau. Wilcox affirmed anticipated charge-off plateau with improvements expected in the second half of 2026.
  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James: Questioned positive operating leverage amid rate cuts. Bailey confirmed the guidance includes a 25 basis point cut. Tamayo asked about margin risks; Wilcox cited expense control as the most manageable lever.
  • Other analysts asked about margin sensitivity, lease income outlook, expense areas of investment, and deposit growth relative to loan growth, with management offering measured, detailed responses.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was generally constructive but focused on risks and sustainability of margin, loan growth, and fee income, pressing for specifics in guidance and risk management.
  • Management maintained a confident and measured tone in prepared remarks and Q&A, using phrases such as “we are optimistic,” “we feel well provisioned,” and “we continue to be encouraging.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remained steady, with a continued emphasis on discipline and patience, while analysts persisted in probing asset quality, loan growth, and capital management.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 net interest margin remained in the 4%–4.2% range, but now includes 125 basis points of anticipated rate cuts, compared to no cuts in the previous quarter’s outlook.
  • Loan growth for 2026 continues to be guided at 3%–5%, consistent with prior commentary.
  • Fee-based income guidance for 2026 was raised to $28–$30 million per quarter from the previous $27–$29 million range.
  • Noninterest expense guidance increased slightly for 2026.
  • Management’s tone and focus on expense control, margin stability, and asset threshold management remained consistent with the previous quarter.
  • Analysts’ questions continued to center on margin dynamics, loan growth, asset quality, M&A strategy, and operational risks.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited continued elevated net charge-offs in the small ticket leasing business, but expects improvement in the second half of 2026.
  • Economic uncertainty and timing of rate cuts pose risks to margin and loan growth.
  • Analysts highlighted potential challenges in deposit growth relative to loan growth and pressed for clarity on provision expectations.
  • Management outlined ongoing expense discipline and strategic balance sheet management to mitigate risks.

Final Takeaway

Peoples Bancorp closed 2025 with stable performance and within guided ranges, highlighting positive operating leverage and improved capital ratios. The company expects 2026 net interest margin of 4%–4.2% and loan growth of 3%–5%, with ongoing management of asset size to avoid crossing the $10 billion threshold absent acquisition activity. Strategic investments in technology and talent, a disciplined approach to M&A, and focused risk management support these targets, while near-term asset quality improvement is anticipated, especially in the leasing portfolio. Management expresses confidence in delivering further shareholder value in the coming year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Trump says, 'You'll find out' how far he'll go to get Greenland
data.description:
When asked how far he would go to gain control of Greenland, President Donald Trump tersely responded, "You'll find out. " The comment came after Trump spent over an hour listing his perceived accomplishments in his second term on Tuesday.

"We have a lot of meetings scheduled on Greenland," he said before leaving to attend the World Economic Forum in Davos. "I think things are going to work out pretty well."

Trump also said he expects he'll be able to send dividends to Americans without Congressional approval. He has floated the idea of sending $2K jobs, subject to some income restrictions.

Developing… check back for updates.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Hawaiian Electric cut to Sell equivalent at Jefferies as price moves ahead of deliverables
data.description:
Hawaiian Electric (HE) -2.9% in Tuesday's trading as Jefferies downgraded shares to Underperform from Hold with a $12.50 price target, trimmed from $13.50, saying the stock's re-rating has "pre-priced success across multiple unresolved gates."

Hawaiian Electric (HE) has climbed from a deep discount to near parity based on a 2028 P/E-based sum of the parts, even though there is still "no implemented alternative rebasing construct, no approved PBR repair package, no numeric liability cap, and no enacted wildfire fund design," analyst Julien Dumoulin-Smith said, adding the stock is now discounting a 2028 earnings and risk profile that has not been earned in the record.

The stock's recent run-up has occurred despite no big fundamental changes to date, Dumoulin-Smith said, which matters "because the market is treating the move as if the fundamental regime has already shifted, when the company is still in process and still negotiating the key economics."

While the core utility business remains integral to Hawaii's energy landscape, the analyst said the uncertainty surrounding potential future wildfire liabilities and how the remainder of the Maui
wildfire tort litigation settlement obligation will be financed remain overhangs.

data.title: Nasdaq plunges more than 2% and has been led lower by these names
data.description:
The technology-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) and the Nasdaq-100 Index (NDX) came under pressure on Tuesday, with the Composite sliding 2.2% on the session.

The pullback underscores a clear shift toward a risk-off posture, as investors reduced exposure across U.S. equities amid renewed geopolitical uncertainty tied to U.S.–Greenland developments. 

Losses were widespread within the Nasdaq-100, where selling dominated much of the index. In total, 77 of its constituents traded lower, while just 23 stocks within the index were in the green. 

The broad retreat reinforces the heightened volatility gripping technology and growth-oriented names, as investor caution continues to dictate market behavior. Below is a closer look at the session’s most pronounced declines among Nasdaq-100 constituents.

  1. Strategy (MSTR) -7.5%
  2. Shopify (SHOP) -7.1%
  3. Broadcom (AVGO) -4.9%
  4. Autodesk (ADSK) -4.8%
  5. KLA Corporation (KLAC) -4.5%
  6. Constellation Energy (CEG) -4.4%
  7. AstraZenica (AZN) -4.4%
  8. Tesla (TSLA) -4%
  9. Nvidia (NVDA) -3.9%
  10. GE HealthCare Technologies (GEHC) -3.8%

Nasdaq Focused ETFs: (QQQ), (QQQM), (SQQQ), (TQQQ), (QLD), and (QID).

data.title: Blackstone evaluating $5B sale of Beacon Offshore - report
data.description:
Blackstone Inc. (BX) is considering a sale of Beacon Offshore Energy that could be valued at more than $5 billion.

The alternative asset manager is in the early stages of evaluating a sale of the Gulf of Mexico driller that it's owned for about a decade, according to a Bloomberg report on Tuesday, which cited people familiar with the matter. Blackstone is talking to investment banks to market the company as soon as Q1.

Beacon is likely to attract attention from larger producers with operations in the Gulf of Mexico, according to the report. Major operators in the Gulf include Chevron (CVX), BP Plc (BP) and Shell Plc (SHEL). No final decision has been made, and Blackstone could decide not to sell, the report said.

Blackstone declined to comment to Bloomberg. A representative for Beacon didn’t immediately have a comment.

data.title: Nasdaq drops 2% as geopolitical jitters rattle Wall Street
data.description:
Wall Street’s major market averages dropped and Treasury yields jumped on Tuesday as the standoff between the U.S. and Greenland continued to heat up.

The tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) fell the hardest as it was last down 2.2%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was lower by 2% on the day, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was down 1.8%. 

From a sector vantage point, all of the S&P 500’s 11 sectors were in negative trading territory, with consumer discretionary sliding the most. Going in the other direction, energy has been the best performer as oil prices are up 1%.

Market volatility also surged back into focus as the Cboe Volatility Index (VIX) climbed above the 20 threshold, marking its highest level in nearly two months. 

Treasury yields jumped higher across the curve as investors sold bonds. The shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) moved up 1 basis point to 3.59%, while the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) ticked up 5 basis points to 4.28%. 

“Tariff fears are back in focus and are now intertwined in geopolitical matters, and not just economic ones. While this adds a new wrinkle to the tariff issue, we believe cooler heads will prevail and that these tariff threats are being used as a negotiating tactic for control of Greenland,” said Paul Stanley, chief investment officer of Granite Bay Wealth Management.

data.title: Albemarle, Lithium Argentina raised to Buy at HSBC as lithium surplus vanishes quickly
data.description:
Albemarle (ALB) +4.1% as HSBC upgraded the top lithium producer and Lithium Argentina (LAR) to Buy from Hold with respective $200 and $9.60 price targets, raised from $117 and $4.75 previously, saying the two stocks show the highest sensitivity to increases in lithium prices.

With lithium demand growth outpacing supply growth, HSBC analysts led by Ishan Jain said they expect the market to turn to a deficit in the medium term, revising their estimated 2026-29 China BG lithium carbonate price forecasts by 12%-48% on expectations of a lower surplus, while also noting that 5% higher demand or 5% lower supply than estimated would be enough to eliminate their 2026 surplus estimate.

BG China lithium carbonate prices have jumped ~180% from the June trough, while spodumene
prices are up 226% from trough levels, and although the trajectory has not been linear, lithium prices are rallying again, and this time the rally looks sustainable, HSBC said.

Albemarle's (ALB) measures to cut capex, raise capital, sell non-core businesses, and foster efficiency are resulting in improved free cash flow generation and balance sheet, HSBC said, adding the stock benefits from the increase in lithium prices on its spot volumes and that the rise in lithium prices should be helpful in contract negotiations when they are due for rollover in the next 18-24 months.

The bank said it continues to prefer SQM (SQM) among lithium producers due to the company's operational excellence, strong balance sheet, and potential volume growth.

HSBC is not the first group to say the tide has turned in the lithium market, as Albemarle (ALB) has enjoyed at least four previous new Buy ratings YTD.

ETFs: (LIT), (BATT)
